lead: Moving day in Newmarket runs best when you have a clear checklist and a little local know-how behind you. This is the global home of horse racing, a CB8 town on the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border where narrow central streets and early-morning gallops activity can catch the unprepared out. Since 2005 our family-run crews have handled countless moves here, and we have learned exactly what makes the difference. Here is our practical moving day checklist, built around the things that actually matter in Newmarket.

sections.body:
A calm moving day in Newmarket starts the evening before. By now the bulk of your packing should be done, so the final night is about the last few jobs rather than a frantic scramble. Strip the beds you are not sleeping in, finish the kitchen down to a single kettle and a few mugs, and make sure every box is sealed and clearly labelled with its destination room written on the side rather than the top.

Set aside an essentials box and keep it in your own car rather than on the lorry. Into it goes the kettle, tea, mugs, phone chargers, basic toiletries, any medication and your important documents. When you arrive at the other end tired and surrounded by sealed boxes, being able to make a cup of tea straight away is worth its weight in gold. It is the smallest item on the list and the one you will thank yourself for most.

Get a proper night's sleep if you can. In Newmarket that is easier said than done if you are near the training yards, as activity around the gallops begins very early. If your new or old home sits close to the main grounds, do not be surprised by the dawn bustle of horses heading out. Better to expect it than to lie awake wondering what the noise is on an already big day.

This is the part of a Newmarket move that catches people out, so it sits near the top of our checklist. Many of the town's older central streets are narrow, with parking restrictions and limited room for a large removals lorry. If your move involves one of these roads, sort the parking well in advance. A clear space directly outside the door can save an hour of carrying boxes the length of a street.

Where parking is genuinely tight, speak to neighbours the day before and ask them to leave a gap if they can. Most people are happy to help when they know a move is happening, and a friendly word goes a long way. If restrictions apply, check whether you need any permission to park the lorry, as the rules vary depending on which side of the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border your address sits.

When we survey a Newmarket move beforehand, access is one of the first things we look at. It lets us choose the right size of vehicle and plan how we will load and unload without blocking the road or upsetting the neighbours. The newer estates around the edges of town are usually far more straightforward, but it never hurts to think the logistics through before the day arrives.

On the morning itself, the aim is simply to be ready when the crew arrives. Have the path to the door clear, the lights on, and the kettle accessible. Walk through the house one last time and pull together any final loose items, because the things left lying about are always the ones that get forgotten. A quick tidy of the hallway makes a real difference to how fast we can load.

Keep children and pets safe and settled, ideally with a friend or relative for the day, or in one quiet room that has already been cleared. With the front door standing open and a constant stream of heavy boxes going past, a busy hallway is no place for a curious toddler or a nervous cat. In a town where the open door might also mean an unexpected view of passing horses, a settled pet is doubly welcome.

Point the crew to anything that needs special care as soon as we arrive, rather than leaving it to chance. If you have fragile pieces, awkward furniture or anything that has to come apart to get down the stairs, tell us up front. Our crews would far rather know early, and everything we move is fully covered, so there is never any reason to hold back on the detail.

sections.h: Reading the Meters and Final Checks
sections.body:
Before you hand over the keys, take dated photographs of every meter with the readings clearly visible. This is one of those thirty-second jobs that settles any billing dispute before it starts, and it matters all the more in Newmarket where the border between Suffolk and Cambridgeshire can mean different suppliers serve different parts of town. A clear photo with the date showing is all the proof you will ever need.

Do a proper walk-through of the whole house once it is empty, and we mean every corner. Check the loft, the shed, the garage and behind every door. Open all the cupboards and look under the stairs. It is astonishing how often something turns up in a forgotten cupboard at the last minute, and far better to find it now than to discover it missing once you have driven away.

Gather all the keys, including window keys, garage keys and any spares, and leave them where you have agreed. If you are leaving instructions for the new occupants about the heating, the bins or anything quirky about the house, a short note left on the side is a kind touch. With Newmarket's council split across two authorities, a quick line about which bins go out and when can be genuinely helpful to whoever follows you.

sections.h: Settling Into Your New Newmarket Home
sections.body:
At the new end, the job is mostly about directing traffic. Stand near the door and tell the crew which room each box belongs in as it comes off the lorry. This is where all that careful labelling pays off, because a well-marked box ends its journey exactly where it belongs rather than in a heap in the hallway. Let us rebuild the beds before we leave, as the difference between a made-up bed and a buried mattress is the difference between a good first night and a miserable one.

Once the lorry has gone, resist the urge to unpack everything at once. Start with the essentials box, get the kettle on, find the bedding, and sort the bathroom. Everything else can wait until the morning. In a new town there is plenty of time to explore, and Newmarket rewards a wander once you have caught your breath, from the High Street to the gallops on the edge of town.

Finally, settle the practical loose ends in your first few days. Confirm which council covers your new address, register for the right bin collections, and update the last of your address changes. Newmarket's border position makes that first check worth doing properly. Get these small things squared away early and you can relax into enjoying the town that draws so many people to this corner of the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire countryside.

Beyond the standard checklist, a handful of details are worth keeping in mind because of Newmarket's particular character. If your move takes you across or alongside the gallops crossings, plan your timing around the early-morning activity where you can. The quiet hours just after dawn are when the strings of racehorses are most active, so a mid-morning move often means clearer roads and an easier run for the lorry through the town.

Think too about the time of year. Newmarket's calendar swells around the racing season, and the town can be noticeably busier at certain points, which affects traffic and parking near the centre. We are not suggesting you build a move around the racing fixtures, but a quick awareness of whether the town is in a busy spell helps explain why some days flow more easily than others. Local knowledge like this is exactly what our years working here have given us.

Lastly, give yourself permission to take it gently once the work is done. Moving is tiring wherever you do it, and a new town takes a little while to feel like home. Newmarket has a gentle, purposeful rhythm that draws people in over time, so do not expect to feel settled overnight. Unpack the essentials, get a good night's sleep in a properly made-up bed, and let the town reveal itself to you over the following days and weeks at its own pace.

faqs: What should I do about parking for a removals lorry in Newmarket?

Sort it early, especially on Newmarket's narrower central streets where parking is restricted. Speak to neighbours the day before, check whether any permission is needed for your side of the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border, and we will plan the right vehicle when we survey the move.
What goes in my essentials box for moving day?

The kettle, tea, mugs, phone chargers, basic toiletries, any medication and important documents. Keep it in your own car rather than on the lorry, so that when you arrive at your new Newmarket home you can make a cup of tea straight away without hunting through sealed boxes.
Why should I photograph the meters when moving in Newmarket?

A dated photo of each meter reading settles any billing dispute before it starts. It matters in Newmarket because the Suffolk and Cambridgeshire border means different suppliers can serve different parts of town, so clear proof of your readings is well worth the thirty seconds it takes.
Will the early-morning gallops affect my moving day in Newmarket?

If your home is near the training yards, expect dawn activity as strings of racehorses head out to the gallops. It will not disrupt the move itself, but it is worth knowing so the early bustle does not catch you off guard on what is already a long and busy day.
